regulations.content: The Bank Secrecy Act (BSA) requires financial institutions to assist U.S. government agencies in detecting and preventing money laundering. Key requirements include filing Currency Transaction Reports (CTRs) for transactions over $10,000 and Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs) for suspicious transactions.

Anti-Money Laundering (AML) compliance programs must include: (1) Internal policies, procedures and controls; (2) Designation of a compliance officer; (3) Ongoing employee training; (4) Independent audit function.

Know Your Customer (KYC) requirements mandate collecting customer identification, verifying identity, understanding the nature of customer's business, and monitoring transactions for suspicious activity.
The Dodd-Frank Act requires enhanced prudential standards for large financial companies, including risk management requirements, stress testing, and early remediation requirements.

risk_factors.content: High-risk transaction indicators include: transactions structured to avoid reporting thresholds, rapid movement of funds through multiple accounts, transactions inconsistent with customer's business, and transactions involving high-risk jurisdictions.
Geographic risk factors include countries identified as having weak AML/CFT regimes, countries subject to sanctions, and countries with high levels of corruption.
Customer risk factors include: Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs), customers in cash-intensive businesses, non-resident customers, and complex ownership structures.

audit_procedures.content: Continuous auditing involves real-time monitoring of transactions, automated alerts for unusual patterns, periodic sampling of transactions, and ongoing risk assessment updates.
Audit sampling methods include: random sampling for low-risk populations, stratified sampling for varied risk levels, and judgmental sampling for high-risk areas.
Key audit procedures for financial institutions include: verifying transaction documentation, testing internal controls, reviewing exception reports, and assessing compliance with regulations.

investigation_guidelines.content: When investigating suspicious transactions, gather all relevant documentation, analyze transaction patterns, interview involved parties, and document all findings thoroughly.
Evidence collection must maintain chain of custody, preserve original documents, and ensure all evidence is admissible in legal proceedings.

reporting_requirements.content: CTRs must be filed within 15 days of the transaction. The report must include customer identification, transaction details, and filing institution information.
SARs must be filed within 30 days of detecting suspicious activity. They require detailed description of the suspicious activity and involved parties.
